title: Fitting laminate flooring: floating method
subtitle: Expansion gap, click and lock, cut the last row
excerpt: How to lay a floating laminate floor from scratch: preparing the subfloor, laying the underlay, establishing the first row, clicking the boards together, cutting around obstacles, and fitting the final row and skirting to cover the expansion gap.
sourceNotes: Standard UK laminate flooring installation practice. Manufacturer guidelines from Pergo and Quick-Step. City & Guilds Floor Laying Level 2 syllabus. The floating-floor principle is unchanged from its introduction in the 1970s.

recipeTools.notes: For checking the subfloor is level before laying.
For smoothing any high spots on the subfloor.

glossaryTerms.definition: The clear space, typically 10 to 12 mm, left between a floating floor and every wall, pipe, and fixed obstacle. The floor expands and contracts with humidity; if it cannot move, it buckles. Covered by the skirting board or scotia bead.

content.text: A floating laminate floor is not fixed to the subfloor. The boards click together to form a single rigid panel that rests on an underlay and moves as a unit with changes in humidity. The most important single requirement is the
at every wall: leave it out and the floor will buckle in warm weather. The second requirement is a clean, level subfloor.
Measure the room area, add 10 percent for wastage, and buy to that quantity. Let the boards acclimatise in the room for 48 hours before fitting.
A 2 to 3 mm foam underlay goes down before the boards. Some boards have underlay pre-attached; check before buying separate underlay.
For maintaining the expansion gap. Commercial plastic spacers or cut-off offcuts both work.

The tapping block fits over the tongue of a board so the rubber mallet drives the click joint home without damaging the edge. The pull bar hooks onto the last board in a row and lets you drive it into the previous board using the mallet.
For cross-cuts, rip cuts, and cuts around door architrave feet.

The subfloor must be clean, dry, and level within 3 mm over any 1800 mm span. Check with the spirit level. Nail down any squeaky or springy floorboards. Fill low spots in concrete with a self-levelling compound; sand high spots back. Sweep and vacuum.

Roll out the underlay with the joins at right angles to the planned board direction. Butt the joins; do not overlap. Tape the joins with the manufacturer's tape. On a concrete subfloor, a damp-proof membrane goes under the underlay if not already built into it.

Lay the first row along the longest wall, with the tongue edge facing out into the room. The last row should be at least 50 mm wide; if the measurement from the first row to the far wall leaves a last row narrower than this, trim the first-row boards to widen the last row. Place spacers against the starting wall to hold the expansion gap.
Step 4, click the first row together.

Click the boards end-to-end along the first row, staggering end joints by at least 300 mm from the previous row. Cut the last board in the row to fit, leaving the expansion gap at the end wall. Keep the offcut if it is longer than 300 mm; it starts the second row and staggers the joints.

Most systems use an angle-and-press method: tilt the new board's long groove edge against the previous row's long tongue at about 30 degrees, then press down to click the long joint home. Then close the short-end joint at one end of the new board by tapping the tapping block with the rubber mallet. Work from left to right along each row.

At pipes, cut a hole slightly larger than the pipe diameter, then cut the board in half to get it around the pipe, and re-join the halves with adhesive. At door architrave feet, undercut the foot with a saw laid flat on a board offcut as a depth guide; the flooring slides underneath.

Measure the gap to the wall at the top and bottom of the last row, allowing for the expansion gap. Rip the boards to width on the jigsaw. Click the long joint of the last row into the previous row using the pull bar and mallet to tap the boards home, since a tapping block cannot be used against a wall.

Remove the spacers once the whole floor is down. Fix new skirting boards or scotia bead to the wall (not to the floor) to cover the expansion gap. Fixing skirting to the floor pins it and prevents movement.
The expansion gap was too small or the boards were not acclimatised before fitting. Remove the skirting, check the gap around the full perimeter, and trim back the boards if necessary.
The end joints were not driven home fully. On a newly laid floor this is correctable while it is still unloaded: reopen the joint and re-click with the tapping block.

Usually caused by the underlay moving and bunching up under the boards, or the boards rubbing a section of the subfloor that was not fully level. Lift the affected boards, re-lay the underlay flat, and re-lay.
